senses.definition: to quickly move forward while also swaying left and right in an uncontrolled and dangerous way

examples.x: The car careened down the winding mountain road, its tires screeching with every sharp turn.
The ship careened in the stormy seas, its hull creaking under the force of the waves.
The cyclist lost control and careened into the oncoming traffic, narrowly avoiding a collision.
The runaway shopping cart careened down the parking lot, leaving a trail of scattered groceries in its wake.
The skier careened down the steep slope, struggling to maintain balance on the icy terrain.

senses.definition: (of a vehicle) to slide or slip uncontrollably, usually on a slippery surface
to move with an unsteady, rocking, or swaying motion, often implying a lack of stability or balance

examples.x: The toddler wobbled as he took his first steps, trying to maintain balance.
The table wobbled on the uneven floor, making it challenging to set a steady cup of coffee.
As the bicycle gained speed, the wheels began to wobble, causing the rider to struggle for control.
The jelly on the plate wobbled with each movement, almost threatening to slide off.
The loose wheel on the shopping cart caused it to wobble as it was pushed through the supermarket.

senses.definition: (of a river, trail, etc.) to follow along a curvy or indirect path

examples.x: The river meanders through the picturesque countryside, creating a serene and scenic landscape.
The stream meanders gently through the meadow, providing a soothing backdrop to the surrounding nature.
The hiking trail meanders up the mountain, offering breathtaking views at every turn.

senses.definition: to run faster than a walk but slower than a full sprint

examples.x: Trying to catch the bus, she trotted down the street, hoping to make it to the stop in time.
The fitness enthusiast trotted around the park as part of their daily jogging routine.
The children excitedly trotted to the ice cream truck when they heard the familiar jingle.
The soccer player trotted across the field, warming up before the game.
Focused on their fitness goals, the group of friends trotted together in the local park.

senses.definition: to tread heavily and forcefully, often with a rhythmic or deliberate motion

examples.x: The toddler gleefully stomped in the puddles after the rain, splashing water everywhere.
The angry elephant showed its displeasure by stomping on the ground with its massive feet.
The angry child proceeded to stomp away from the playground.
During the tribal dance, participants would stomp in a ritualistic manner to connect with the earth.
The teacher stomped towards the chalkboard to get everyone's attention.

senses.definition: to move quickly and with short, hasty steps

examples.x: The spy scuttled through the dimly lit alley, trying to avoid detection.
The child, excited to explore the garden, scuttled around, chasing butterflies.
Frightened by the barking dog, the squirrel scuttled up the tree to safety.
Trying to catch the last train, commuters scuttled along the crowded platform.
The cat scuttled across the roof, disappearing from view in seconds.

senses.definition: to perform a gymnastic move involving rolling the body sideways in a full circle, typically with arms and legs extended

examples.x: The gymnast effortlessly cartwheeled across the mat, showcasing her agility and grace.
Inspired by the circus performer, the child attempted to cartwheel in the backyard.
After scoring the winning goal, the soccer player couldn't contain his joy and cartwheeled in celebration.
Delighted by the news, he cartwheeled out of the office, thrilled about the unexpected promotion.
The playful puppy cartwheeled in the backyard, reveling in the freedom of the open space.

senses.definition: to twist, turn, or move with quick, contorted motions

examples.x: The playful puppy wriggled with excitement as its owner approached with a treat.
The toddler couldn't sit still and continuously wriggled in her chair during the family dinner.
As the fishing line tightened, the fish began to wriggle, attempting to free itself from the hook.
Feeling ticklish, the child couldn't help but giggle and wriggle when their friend poked his side.
As the magician escaped from the straitjacket, the audience watched in amazement as he wriggled free.

senses.definition: to perform a gymnastic or acrobatic movement in which the body makes a complete revolution, typically forwards or backwards, with the feet passing over the head

examples.x: The gymnast somersaulted gracefully across the mat, showcasing her agility and skill.
The acrobat somersaulted through the air, executing a series of flips and twists in the breathtaking routine.
In the thrilling finale, the circus performer somersaulted through a ring of fire, leaving the crowd in awe.
Filled with enthusiasm, the kids somersaulted and tumbled in the gymnastics class.
The trapeze artist elegantly somersaults from one bar to another, captivating the audience below.

senses.definition: to move quickly and lightly from somewhere or something to another

examples.x: Butterflies flit gracefully from flower to flower in the garden.
Memories of her childhood flit through her mind like fleeting shadows.

senses.definition: to dance, move, or skip with quick, lively steps

examples.x: The group of friends decided to jig around the bonfire, adding a festive atmosphere to the celebration.
During the carnival parade, participants dressed in colorful costumes jigged along the route, entertaining spectators.
The children are jigging to the catchy tune playing on the radio.

senses.definition: to move swiftly and abruptly in a particular direction

examples.x: Startled by the sudden noise, the cat darted across the room and hid under the furniture.
Trying to catch the bus, she darted through the crowded station.
Faced with an approaching storm, the pedestrians darted for cover.
The rabbit darted into the bushes when it sensed the presence of a predator.
The child, excited to join the game, darted towards the playground equipment.

senses.definition: to pull something or someone along the ground, usually with difficulty

examples.x: The workers hauled the large crates across the warehouse floor to the loading dock.
He hauled the injured hiker down the mountain trail, struggling with the rough terrain.
The sailors hauled the anchor aboard, straining under its weight.
The farmer hauled the sacks of potatoes across the muddy field to the barn.
It took two people to haul the heavy boulder out of the way.

senses.definition: to move smoothly and quietly, like a snake

examples.x: The snake silently slithered through the grass.
The lizard deftly slithered over the rocks, blending seamlessly with its surroundings.
The river serpent slithered along the muddy bank.
The frost-covered snake slithered across the icy path.

senses.definition: to turn or move around an axis or center
to climb a surface using hands and feet

examples.x: The cat tried to clamber onto the high shelf to reach its favorite perch.
The children eagerly clambered up the tree to retrieve their stuck kite.
In the dense forest, the hiker had to clamber up a steep slope to continue on the trail.
The agile squirrel easily clambered up the tall tree trunk to access the bird feeder.
To escape the rising floodwaters, the family had to clamber onto the roof of their house.

senses.definition: to move in a loose, uncontrolled, or erratic manner

examples.x: The fish flopped around on the deck of the boat after being caught.
The kite began to flop in the strong wind, making it difficult for the child to control.
After a long run, the exhausted runner's legs started to flop as they struggled to maintain pace.
The old flag, worn by the elements, would flop in the breeze, its edges tattered and torn.
The comedian's exaggerated gestures caused his arms to flop comically during the performance.

senses.definition: to move or run away quickly and unexpectedly

examples.x: Startled by the loud noise, the deer bolted into the nearby woods.
As the thunderstorm approached, the cat bolted under the bed in fear.
The dog, frightened by the fireworks, bolted out of the backyard and into the street.
In the chaotic scene, people began to bolt from the crowded concert venue.

senses.definition: to fall or drop with a soft, muffled sound

examples.x: The raindrops began to plop on the leaves, creating a soothing rhythm in the forest.
As the fish leaped out of the water, it would plop back in with a splash.
The frog leaped into the pond, and a moment later, it could be heard plopping into the water.
The melting ice cream fell from the cone and plopped onto the sidewalk.

senses.definition: to move rapidly

examples.x: The athlete effortlessly zipped through the obstacle course, showcasing remarkable agility.
The car zipped along the highway, covering miles in a short amount of time.
The magician appeared to zip from one side of the stage to the other in the blink of an eye.
As the motorcycle zipped through traffic, it left a trail of speed behind.
The child excitedly zipped up and down the playground slide, full of energy.

senses.definition: to move quickly and lightly in a particular direction or manner

examples.x: The ballerina gracefully whisked across the stage, capturing the audience's attention.
The cat whisked through the narrow alley, chasing a playful butterfly.
The speeding car whisked past, leaving a trail of dust behind on the country road.
The dandelion seeds whisked into the air.

senses.definition: to move swiftly in a specified direction, leaving a visible trail or mark

examples.x: With a burst of speed, the athlete streaked past the finish line, securing the victory.
The car streaked down the highway, its headlights piercing through the darkness.
The cyclist streaks down the hill, wind rushing past as the wheels spin.
The hummingbird streaks through the garden, hovering momentarily at each flower.
The laser pointer streaks through the air, highlighting key points on the presentation.

senses.definition: to get stuck in mud or wet ground, preventing movement
to walk with short, clumsy steps and a swaying motion from side to side, typically as a result of being overweight or having short legs

examples.x: The penguin waddled across the ice, its flippers outstretched for balance.
After a long day of hiking, his legs felt so sore that he could only waddle back to the campsite.
The toddler waddled around the room, giggling with delight at his newfound ability to walk.
The ducklings waddled behind their mother as she led them to the pond for their first swim.
Due to the heavy backpack, she had to waddle up the steep hill, taking small, careful steps to maintain her balance.
